source_review.summary: Mechanics pass and all five score-driving sources resolve. The sources support the stated MSP adoption, pricing, consolidation, valuation, and succession context, but the thesis is intentionally conditional on an MSP/system-integrator subsegment and uses broad, vendor-sponsored, advisory, and adjacent-code proxies. The record preserves those limitations and separates near-term capture from terminal paid demand, so they warrant caveats rather than rejection.

sources.title: Kaseya, 2026 State of the MSP Report press release
sources.what_it_supports: Survey of more than 1,000 MSPs worldwide: 53% already use AI for ticketing, patching and monitoring; 48% rank AI/automation as the leading client need; cybersecurity and business-continuity revenue are growing. Used for operational adoption and terminal-demand context.

sources.title: Kaseya, MSP Pricing: A Guide to Managed IT Services Pricing
sources.what_it_supports: MSP pricing modes include per-user, per-device, combined, tiered and fixed-fee subscriptions; the source also explains that per-user revenue fluctuates with client employee counts. Its benchmark figures are from the 2023 survey, so pricing evidence is older than adoption evidence.

sources.title: Solganick & Co., Technology Services M&A Market Update, 1H 2025
sources.what_it_supports: MSP M&A activity rebounded in Q2 2025; private-equity interest remained robust for predictable/scalable revenue, and the report lists current platform acquisitions including an Evergreen Lyra MSP deal. Its advisory focus is firms with roughly $2M–$20M EBITDA, broader than every target in the screen.

sources.title: Aventis Advisors, MSP Valuation Multiples
sources.what_it_supports: Advisor analysis says smaller MSPs typically trade around 5–8x EBITDA, while larger MSPs receive higher multiples; its disclosed-transaction median is 8.9x but the median deal size is $38.5M. Used for conservative buy/exit assumptions and size-discount caveats.

sources.title: Excendio Advisors, Navigating Growth, Exit & Legacy as an MSP Owner
sources.what_it_supports: MSP-focused exit adviser describes a large cohort of entrepreneurial founders from the 1990s and early 2000s, many now in their 50s or older, and discusses consolidation and transition challenges. It is advisory commentary, not a representative owner-age census.

source_review.summary: Mechanics, identity, model routing, digest binding, deterministic dataset injection, fresh finalization, fresh memo rendering, and arithmetic all pass. All five registered score-driving sources are accessible. They support the useful direction of the workflow, licensing, consolidation, and valuation evidence, while the selected adoption, automation, capture, ownership, target-specific multiple, and future-exit judgments remain disclosed estimates or broad proxies. Factor semantics are not reversed, and no terminal/capture or ownership/consolidation reuse is likely to change the thesis: the no-re-rating 8.0x entry/exit case sets M to zero and independently preserves the kill verdict. The record is therefore publishable with its fifteen authored caveats.

source_review.summary: Mechanics, exact identity/model routing, manifest digests, dataset injection, arithmetic, fresh finalization and fresh memo rendering all pass. All six registered sources are accessible. The direct adoption, adviser-population, deal-volume, buyer-count, assignment-consent and 11.0x adjusted-EBITDA claims are supported; remaining weaknesses are disclosed estimates or non-fatal source/scope limitations. No reversed factor semantics or capture/terminal double count is likely to change the thesis: client-consent risk is treated as a transferability constraint, and the PRESSURED terminal cap does not bind the current conditional score. The useful conclusion remains publishable only with all 18 authored caveats plus the two validator caveats, especially because M, n_band, owner age and capture can move the near-cut result.

material_findings.materiality: pi_dist enters C linearly. Holding pi_moat at 0.55, a selected pi_dist of 0.25 or 0.75 would move C from 2.75 to 1.375 or 4.125. Because S1 does not anchor the selected value or its direction, the capture factor can change materially even though the current zero M factor still forces the overall kill verdict.

Separating survival of paid licensed work from an operator's ability to retain AI-created savings could materially change pi_moat and therefore C while leaving terminal demand DURABLE. For example, a 0.20 change in pi_moat moves C by 1.0 at the selected pi_dist, so this prohibited capture/terminal overlap can materially change a factor.

Holding the other recorded inputs fixed, changing owners_60plus_pct from 0.30 to 0.10 changes B from 6.12 to about 2.56; changing the unsupported consolidator count also changes the crowding penalty. The ungrounded selections therefore can materially change the buyability factor.

source_review.summary: Reject. Exact packet/run/memo identity, schema, model route, dataset injection, fresh finalization, fresh memo rendering, digests, and arithmetic all pass. S4 supports the remediated 4.92x entry benchmark and the no-expansion exit is clearly labeled as an estimate. However, the capture estimate lacks relevant pricing evidence, licensing is used for both capture and terminal demand without distinct channels, and the ownership/consolidator inputs that drive B are unsupported; each defect can materially change a factor.

source_review.summary: All deterministic mechanics and frozen digests pass, and all six registered sources are live. The useful kill thesis is supported by observed legal GenAI adoption, documented workflow exposure, and binding ownership constraints. Publication requires the authored caveats because owner demographics, target-band transaction multiples, consolidator count, practice mix, and several economic selections rely on disclosed broad proxies or estimates; none is an inaccessible or contradicted score-driving claim that warrants rejection.

source_review.summary: All deterministic mechanics, exact-byte digests, identities, route, dataset injection, fresh finalization, memo rendering, and arithmetic pass. Reopened sources support the useful direction of AI task compression, partial savings pass-through, limited workflow/data capture, adoption timing, active A/E acquisition, valuation ranges, and durable accountable demand. The record separates licensure and responsible review as terminal-demand mechanisms from pi_moat, so no score-changing terminal/capture double count remains. Publication nevertheless requires the authored caveats because several score inputs rely on disclosed broader-AEC proxies, unquantified contract mix and capture, an unobserved owner-age/shortage estimate, and adviser or paywalled valuation evidence.

caveats: inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The estimate treats AI scribes as documentation augmentation and caps DVM substitution well below administrative work.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The supplied mix is 5-digit 54194 and includes laboratories and specialty care as well as GP clinics; role-level fractions are a companion-clinic investment view.
inputs.pi_dist: Insured-pet counts are not a clinic-level payer-mix measure and do not establish local pricing power.
inputs.pi_moat: Federal VCPR rules do not create a universal in-person requirement; state law and the service line matter.
inputs.t50_years: The survey is a voluntary specialty-membership sample, not a representative general-practice adoption census.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: This is a specialty-survey proxy, not a measured adoption rate for all NAICS 541940 operators.
inputs.historical_analogs: No audited historical AI adoption curve for independent GP clinics was found in the reopened sources.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: No reopened source provided a current national 541940 owner-over-60 percentage; diligence must replace this estimate with target-level owner age and succession intent.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct.succession_shortage_documented: Corporate consolidation evidence is not itself proof that each owner lacks an internal successor.
inputs.active_consolidators: This is a screening count, not a verified current deal pipeline or a count of all buyers.
inputs.buy_mult: The source reports asking-price multiples of seller's discretionary earnings, not closed transactions or adjusted EBITDA; owner-DVM replacement must be normalized in diligence.
inputs.exit_mult: The exit multiple is not supported as an EBITDA platform multiple; it deliberately avoids an expansion thesis.
inputs.pricing_structure: Neither source measures a specific target's realized payer mix or price elasticity.

S4 reports any current AI use, not routine use: 42% of ACVIM and 33% of ECVIM-CA respondents used AI, while 50% and 37% of those users, respectively, reported daily use; the selected 0.38 effective-adoption proxy may therefore be high.

S5 documents consolidation but not a shortage of successors; treating inputs.owners_60plus_pct.succession_shortage_documented as true raises B by about 0.34 points without changing S or the kill verdict.

a.rationale: Weight the supplied code-specific SOC mix by task, not headcount: physical and licensed clinical work is mostly augmented, while communications, documentation, scheduling, and back-office work are materially automatable. The resulting share is an estimate of replaceable task cost, not of eliminated clinical positions.

rho.rationale: Fixed outcome-blind transform of the v3 t50 adoption estimate, widened by the declared bridge error.

publication_caveats: inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The estimate treats AI scribes as documentation augmentation and caps DVM substitution well below administrative work.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The supplied mix is 5-digit 54194 and includes laboratories and specialty care as well as GP clinics; role-level fractions are a companion-clinic investment view.
inputs.pi_dist: Insured-pet counts are not a clinic-level payer-mix measure and do not establish local pricing power.
inputs.pi_moat: Federal VCPR rules do not create a universal in-person requirement; state law and the service line matter.
inputs.t50_years: The survey is a voluntary specialty-membership sample, not a representative general-practice adoption census.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: This is a specialty-survey proxy, not a measured adoption rate for all NAICS 541940 operators.
inputs.historical_analogs: No audited historical AI adoption curve for independent GP clinics was found in the reopened sources.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: No reopened source provided a current national 541940 owner-over-60 percentage; diligence must replace this estimate with target-level owner age and succession intent.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct.succession_shortage_documented: Corporate consolidation evidence is not itself proof that each owner lacks an internal successor.
inputs.active_consolidators: This is a screening count, not a verified current deal pipeline or a count of all buyers.
inputs.buy_mult: The source reports asking-price multiples of seller's discretionary earnings, not closed transactions or adjusted EBITDA; owner-DVM replacement must be normalized in diligence.
inputs.exit_mult: The exit multiple is not supported as an EBITDA platform multiple; it deliberately avoids an expansion thesis.
inputs.pricing_structure: Neither source measures a specific target's realized payer mix or price elasticity.

S4 reports any current AI use, not routine use: 42% of ACVIM and 33% of ECVIM-CA respondents used AI, while 50% and 37% of those users, respectively, reported daily use; the selected 0.38 effective-adoption proxy may therefore be high.

S5 documents consolidation but not a shortage of successors; treating inputs.owners_60plus_pct.succession_shortage_documented as true raises B by about 0.34 points without changing S or the kill verdict.
source_review.summary: Publishable with caveats. All deterministic mechanics and exact-artifact checks pass, every registered source resolves, and the central thesis remains useful. The source audit confirms the clinical-task floor, consolidation, low insurance penetration, and listing-multiple evidence, while preserving the authored scope and proxy warnings. Two additional nonfatal findings correct the adoption-survey wording and flag that documented successor scarcity was not established.

inputs.pi_moat: The source includes managed services and global systems integrators, which often have stickier contracts than a project-oriented 541511 firm.
inputs.pi_moat: This is a capture input only and does not use human accountability to claim a terminal-demand floor.
inputs.t50_years: Tool use and self-reported productivity are not identical to effective adoption across all billable tasks.
inputs.t50_years: The evidence is not a small-firm, NAICS-specific longitudinal adoption measure.

source_review.summary: Mechanics pass and all seven score-driving sources resolve. The sources support the thesis direction and cited market facts, while the packet explicitly discloses that adoption, pricing, buyer, and multiple evidence is drawn from broader developer, ADM, global-provider, UK IT-services, or four-digit occupational frames. Those limitations warrant publication caveats but do not demonstrate a hidden score-changing defect, reversed factor, or capture/terminal double count.

source_review.summary: Mechanics pass. The sources support a technician-heavy, durable physical-service thesis with bounded office and routing automation, active consolidation, and the stated multiple table. Disclosed survey, owner-age, labor-mix, buyer-depth, and transaction-comparability limitations remain non-fatal; licensing should not add a separate capture benefit without distinct evidence.

The sources support the direction of the cautious thesis and the observed numeric anchors; the broad-sector, occupation, ownership, fee-model, and transaction proxies are explicitly disclosed and could move individual factors but do not conceal a score-changing contradiction or reverse the kill/pressured conclusion. caveats: inputs.ai_replaceable_share: S2 is biopharma QC, not US independent 541380 testing laboratories.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The supplied role mix is 5-digit 54138 and leaves a 0.5997 residual wage share; the residual allocation is necessarily broad.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Physical robotics and LLM-class AI are distinct; these estimates do not credit physical automation as immediate AI replacement.
inputs.pi_dist: No US 541380 fee-schedule or margin study was found that isolates AI savings pass-through.
inputs.pi_dist: S8 is UK TIC commentary, while S1 is one regulated environmental-testing program; neither establishes an industry-wide US pricing model.
inputs.pi_moat: This scores retention of savings, not terminal demand; the regulatory human/physical floor is addressed separately in terminal demand.
inputs.pi_moat: Accreditation scope, customer concentration, geography, and test category vary materially across the code.
inputs.t50_years: No representative AI-adoption survey for independent US 541380 laboratories was found.
inputs.t50_years: S2 surveys biopharma executives and may overstate digitization capacity relative to lower-middle-market commercial labs.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: The cited survey is adjacent and is not a measurement of the target NAICS or of labor displacement.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: Generic business AI-use surveys are not used as a substitute for effective lab-workflow adoption.
inputs.historical_analogs: This is an analogy, not a quantitative causal estimate or a 541380 historical time series.
inputs.historical_analogs: Biopharma QC has different regulation, scale, and capital budgets.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: No extracted Census tabulation for NAICS 541380 and the $1-10M EBITDA band was available in the reviewed source.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: S4 is a broad bank-sponsored owner survey, not an industry ownership census.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct.succession_shortage_documented: This does not mean a shortage cannot exist; it means it was not documented by the reviewed evidence for this score input.
inputs.active_consolidators: The sources cover distinct subsegments and are not a comprehensive count of all buyers in 541380.
inputs.active_consolidators: A named acquirer is evidence for buyability only, not for AI task replacement or adoption speed.
inputs.buy_mult: S7 is an advisory-marketing source, not a disclosed-deal database.
inputs.buy_mult: Enterprise-value and revenue thresholds do not exactly map to the supplied $1-10M EBITDA target, and lab capex or customer concentration can materially change the multiple.
inputs.exit_mult: S8 is UK TIC coverage; its deal range, currency, legal environment, and mix are not a US small-laboratory comp set.
inputs.exit_mult: No multiple expansion is assumed in capture or terminal-demand inputs; it is confined to this multiple input.
inputs.pricing_structure: The mix across environmental, materials, food, calibration, product safety, and other 541380 labs is unknown.
inputs.pricing_structure: S8 is UK sector commentary and S1 is a specific US lead-testing example, not a full US pricing survey.

q.rationale: The existing v3 distribution-retention × moat-retention judgment is reused as a bounded Stage 1 commercial-retention proxy.

publication_caveats: inputs.ai_replaceable_share: S2 is biopharma QC, not US independent 541380 testing laboratories.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The supplied role mix is 5-digit 54138 and leaves a 0.5997 residual wage share; the residual allocation is necessarily broad.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Physical robotics and LLM-class AI are distinct; these estimates do not credit physical automation as immediate AI replacement.
inputs.pi_dist: No US 541380 fee-schedule or margin study was found that isolates AI savings pass-through.
inputs.pi_dist: S8 is UK TIC commentary, while S1 is one regulated environmental-testing program; neither establishes an industry-wide US pricing model.
inputs.pi_moat: This scores retention of savings, not terminal demand; the regulatory human/physical floor is addressed separately in terminal demand.
inputs.pi_moat: Accreditation scope, customer concentration, geography, and test category vary materially across the code.
inputs.t50_years: No representative AI-adoption survey for independent US 541380 laboratories was found.
inputs.t50_years: S2 surveys biopharma executives and may overstate digitization capacity relative to lower-middle-market commercial labs.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: The cited survey is adjacent and is not a measurement of the target NAICS or of labor displacement.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: Generic business AI-use surveys are not used as a substitute for effective lab-workflow adoption.
inputs.historical_analogs: This is an analogy, not a quantitative causal estimate or a 541380 historical time series.
inputs.historical_analogs: Biopharma QC has different regulation, scale, and capital budgets.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: No extracted Census tabulation for NAICS 541380 and the $1-10M EBITDA band was available in the reviewed source.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: S4 is a broad bank-sponsored owner survey, not an industry ownership census.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct.succession_shortage_documented: This does not mean a shortage cannot exist; it means it was not documented by the reviewed evidence for this score input.
inputs.active_consolidators: The sources cover distinct subsegments and are not a comprehensive count of all buyers in 541380.
inputs.active_consolidators: A named acquirer is evidence for buyability only, not for AI task replacement or adoption speed.
inputs.buy_mult: S7 is an advisory-marketing source, not a disclosed-deal database.
inputs.buy_mult: Enterprise-value and revenue thresholds do not exactly map to the supplied $1-10M EBITDA target, and lab capex or customer concentration can materially change the multiple.
inputs.exit_mult: S8 is UK TIC coverage; its deal range, currency, legal environment, and mix are not a US small-laboratory comp set.
inputs.exit_mult: No multiple expansion is assumed in capture or terminal-demand inputs; it is confined to this multiple input.
inputs.pricing_structure: The mix across environmental, materials, food, calibration, product safety, and other 541380 labs is unknown.
inputs.pricing_structure: S8 is UK sector commentary and S1 is a specific US lead-testing example, not a full US pricing survey.
source_review.summary: Mechanics and exact-artifact binding pass. All eight registered sources support the claims attributed to them, and the packet keeps the biopharma, broad-owner, adviser, UK-TIC, and subsegment limitations explicit. The useful conclusion remains supportable, but the numerous authored proxy, scope, and uncertainty caveats require publication with caveats.

sources.title: The National Lead Laboratory Accreditation Program (NLLAP) | US EPA
sources.what_it_supports: An official example of a regulated testing segment: accredited laboratories must demonstrate analytical capability, undergo onsite assessment, and continue proficiency testing; specified lead samples must be analyzed by an EPA-recognized lab.

sources.title: Pharma’s QC lab of the future: Boosting speed, compliance, and quality
sources.what_it_supports: A 2025 survey of 103 biopharma executives reports investment in lab automation, AI, data capture, sample management, and traceability, but also substantial disconnected systems and limited automation. It is an adjacent, more capitalized population.

sources.title: ABS - Characteristics of Business Owners: 2022 Tables (Employer Businesses)
sources.what_it_supports: Census makes an employer-business owner-age table available, establishing the appropriate broad US ownership-demographic source; this packet does not claim a 541380-specific extraction from it.

sources.title: BofA Report: 74% of Small and Mid-Sized Business Owners Expect Revenue to Increase in the Next Year
sources.what_it_supports: A weighted 2025 US owner survey reports that 40% lack a succession plan, but its broad size and industry coverage does not document a testing-laboratory-specific shortage.

sources.title: UES Expands into Indiana with Acquisition of GME Testing
sources.what_it_supports: Direct 2025 evidence of a national buyer acquiring a 23-person firm providing geotechnical engineering, construction-materials testing, and foundation inspection/testing.

sources.title: Intertek strengthens its products testing business with acquisition of leading flooring products testing company
sources.what_it_supports: Direct 2025 evidence of a global TIC buyer acquiring a 26-employee US product-testing laboratory.

sources.title: M&A Advisor for TIC Companies | Aventis Advisors
sources.what_it_supports: A TIC M&A advisory source says smaller regional labs below $25M enterprise value typically trade at 6–8x EBITDA; it is adviser marketing rather than a transaction database.

sources.title: Testing, Inspection and Certification: Why resilience continues to attract consolidation
sources.what_it_supports: UK TIC market commentary reports recurring and regulatory demand, transaction activity, and a disclosed-multiple range of 7.5–12.5x; it is useful directionally but is not a US small-laboratory comp set.

source_review.summary: Publishable with caveats. Exact artifact identity, schemas, dataset injection, fresh finalization, memo rendering, and arithmetic all pass. All five registered sources are accessible and support the broad claims attributed to them, but the heterogeneous code, broad adoption and owner evidence, and non-code-specific market proxies make the factor values low-confidence estimates rather than precise 541690 measurements.

source_review.summary: Mechanics pass exactly and all six score-driving sources were accessible when reopened. The sources support the useful directional thesis that environmental consulting has durable regulated and field-dependent work, meaningful document and data automation opportunities, partial capture, and active consolidation, but the score relies on openly disclosed 5416, AEC, ESG, and occupational proxies plus several unmeasured estimates. Those limitations warrant publication caveats but do not constitute a material research defect or reverse the thesis.

sources.title: All Appropriate Inquiries Rule: Definition of Environmental Professional | US EPA

caveats: inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Sources measure client-side supply-chain adoption and applications, not task shares inside NAICS 541614 consultancies.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The supplied role mix is four-digit 5416 and likely understates logisticians, industrial engineers and field implementation staff.

publication_caveats: inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Sources measure client-side supply-chain adoption and applications, not task shares inside NAICS 541614 consultancies.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The supplied role mix is four-digit 5416 and likely understates logisticians, industrial engineers and field implementation staff.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Adoption pace, fee capture and terminal demand are not used to inflate these task fractions.
inputs.pi_dist: No source measures the fee-model mix for the target EBITDA band or this NAICS code.
inputs.pi_dist: Gainshare prevalence in freight audit is not quantified here and therefore receives no direct observed weight.
inputs.pi_moat: Equiteq describes preferred consulting attributes broadly rather than measuring logistics-consulting switching costs.
inputs.pi_moat: Physical implementation supports terminal demand separately and is not itself counted as capture.
inputs.t50_years: Client-side adoption is a proxy for consultant delivery adoption.
inputs.t50_years: The 82% figure is a five-year expectation, not an observed diffusion curve.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: This is observed for surveyed supply-chain organizations, not US lower-middle-market 541614 consulting firms.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: Use of any AI is broader than effective adoption across consulting delivery tasks.
inputs.historical_analogs: This qualitative analog does not set role fractions or adoption timing by itself.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: This is not evidence of a sector succession wave.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct.succession_shortage_documented: Individual firms may still have material succession risk.
inputs.active_consolidators: This is not a direct count of bidders for 541614 firms.
inputs.active_consolidators: Used only for buyability, not adoption or task exposure.
inputs.buy_mult: BizBuySell's 2.72x service-business figure is discretionary earnings for much smaller firms, not EBITDA for the target band.
inputs.buy_mult: Partner compensation, earnouts and cyclicality require normalization.
inputs.exit_mult: No direct 541614 transaction multiple supports the selected expansion.
inputs.exit_mult: Exit uplift is conditional on reduced founder dependence and stronger recurring or gainshare revenue.
inputs.pricing_structure: No direct evidence quantifies gainshare, fixed-fee or T&M revenue in NAICS 541614.
Validator: Supply-chain operator adoption and the four-digit 5416 role mix support direction, not six-digit 541614 consultant task shares or diffusion timing.
Validator: The six-buyer estimate and 6.0x entry/7.5x exit case are judgmental assumptions; the cited M&A evidence supplies only broad direction and a mismatched micro-market comparator.
Validator: Separate contract/data-based retention from the survival of human implementation work before treating the 0.45 moat estimate as capture.
source_review.summary: Mechanics and exact-artifact binding pass. The directional logistics-consulting thesis remains useful, but consultant-specific adoption, fee mix, buyer count, transaction multiples, and capture-versus-terminal separation require visible caveats.

Reopened evidence supports the 39% adoption anchor, task-direction thesis, ERISA credential floor and the disclosed 6.2x public EBITDA reference. source_review.summary: Mechanics, identity, digests, fresh finalization, memo rendering, dataset injection, and arithmetic all pass. The source set supports the directional selective-buyout thesis and the material observed numbers, while the packet visibly limits client-marketing, broad-consulting, national-owner, and cash-flow/SDE proxies. The Salesforce edition drift is a non-material publication caveat. Capture, adoption, ownership, consolidation, and terminal-demand mechanisms are separated without material double counting.

source_review.summary: Publishable with caveats. All deterministic mechanics and exact-artifact checks pass and all seven registered sources resolve. The sources support the residual-code boundary, appraisal-standard qualifications, weather-model capability, broad service-business multiples, and general owner-age context. publication_caveats: inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Role mix is supplied at five-digit 62134 rather than six-digit 621340.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Clinical-task fractions exclude a claim that AI replaces billable hands-on treatment.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Audiology and other residual subsegments may have different exposure.
inputs.pi_dist: CMS Medicare mechanics are a direct payer channel but do not describe every commercial or Medicaid contract.
inputs.pi_moat: Sector consolidation evidence does not prove moat for a clinic whose referrals are physician-personal or payer-concentrated.
inputs.t50_years: Vendor survey language indicates direction, not a representative effective-adoption series.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: No reopened source provides a code-specific percentage of clinics using AI effectively.
inputs.historical_analogs: The source is a vendor survey release and not a causal clinical-productivity study.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: Workforce age and owner age are different populations.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct.succession_shortage_documented: Do not convert therapist vacancy evidence into owner-succession evidence.
inputs.active_consolidators: Platform count does not establish active acquisition appetite for every local specialty or payer mix.

inputs.buy_mult: S4 comparables are materially larger and mostly historical, so this is a conservative bounded estimate rather than an observed band-size transaction multiple. Verify local transaction data and normalize owner-therapist pay.
inputs.exit_mult: The cited transactions had $12M-$175M EBITDA and cannot support a precise multiple for a smaller clinic group.
inputs.pricing_structure: CMS is not a full description of commercial, Medicaid, school or audiology economics.
source_review.summary: Mechanics and exact-artifact binding pass. Reopened sources support the payer-capture channel, administrative AI surface, licensed clinical floor, staffing pressure and active consolidation direction, while the scorecard keeps clinical replacement, capture and terminal demand separate. The pass thesis remains useful, but adoption and small-clinic multiple values are disclosed estimates anchored to vendor evidence and much larger historical transactions, so all authored caveats must remain visible.

source_review.summary: All deterministic mechanics pass and every registered source was accessible. The cautious kill thesis remains useful: sources support the direction of gradual AI adoption, durable physical and regulated work, mixed pricing mechanics, and broad biopharma-services deal activity. The score is nevertheless built from disclosed, low-confidence proxies for small-CRO adoption, owner age, buyer count, contract mix, and entry/exit multiples; these are publication caveats rather than material defects because the proxy limitations are explicit and the conclusion is conservative.

source_review.summary: Mechanics, exact identity, frozen digests, deterministic dataset injection, fresh finalization, memo rendering, and arithmetic all pass. All seven registered sources reopened and support the limited claims made. The thesis remains useful, but the extensive authored caveats correctly identify broad or foreign proxies, a contaminated occupation mix, an anomalous labor-share construction, and weak code-specific ownership and valuation evidence.

sources.title: A survey of generative AI adoption amongst industrial design experts
sources.what_it_supports: A 2026 Design Research Society paper based on interviews with 20 experienced industrial designers describes rapid bottom-up adoption under speed pressure and risks from cognitive offloading and loss of control over design micro-decisions.

sources.title: The State of AI in Design: What we learned from 400+ designers
sources.what_it_supports: A Foundation Capital and Designer Fund survey of nearly 400 designers reports grassroots AI adoption, 96% self-taught tool discovery, and faster adoption at startups than large firms. Its population is broad design, not industrial-design consultancies.

sources.title: How Much Do Product Design Firms Charge?
sources.what_it_supports: A product-design consultancy describes fixed-price, fee-for-service, and fee-plus-equity models, including fixed fees for defined work and staged or hourly arrangements for exploratory outsourced physical-product development.

sources.title: Delve - Industrial Designers Society of America
sources.what_it_supports: An IDSA design-firm directory entry describes a US product-development consultancy combining user research, industrial design, human factors, engineering, prototyping, testing, and in-house prototype development. It is an example, not an industry census.

sources.title: Landing LUNAR
sources.what_it_supports: McKinsey's 2015 announcement says its acquisition of industrial-design firm LUNAR added design execution to its product-development advisory practice. Terms were undisclosed and the transaction is historical.

sources.title: Fen Technology announces the acquisition of Wright Design

sources.title: Middle Market M&A Valuations Index

source_review.summary: Publishable with caveats. Exact-artifact mechanics, deterministic dataset equality, fresh finalization, memo rendering and arithmetic all pass. The reopened sources support the directional kill thesis: AI assists seismic processing and interpretation while expert geological input and physical acquisition remain, small firms compete through specialization, TGS's library economics are a deliberately bounded large-provider contrast, and the TGS-PGS merger is only one top-end event. Numeric adoption, capture, moat, ownership and multiple inputs remain plainly labeled low-confidence estimates; the disclosed uncertainties and the S3 URL-stability issue are non-fatal and do not reverse a factor or change the thesis.

All nine registered sources were reopened; the score-driving sources support the limited claims made, and the packet openly treats promotional-products distribution, adoption, role mix and valuation as scoped proxies or estimates. source_review.summary: All deterministic mechanics and exact-artifact checks pass. Every registered source is accessible; the central market, regulatory, attorney-opinion and valuation figures are present on the cited pages. The remaining weaknesses are disclosed AI/adoption and consolidation proxies, plus the thirteen authored caveats. The attorney-opinion mechanism is used for present order-flow ownership in C and long-run paid-demand pressure in the terminal classification, not as a demonstrated thesis-changing double count; in any event the independently supported no-expansion assumption sets M to zero and determines the kill verdict. The record is useful and publishable with caveats.

inputs.t50_years: AAPOR does not report a representative current-adoption rate or diffusion curve for US lower-middle-market 541720 firms.
inputs.t50_years: This is delivery adoption, not client enthusiasm or a count of experimental AI use.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: No source in this packet directly measures current adoption for the target industry, geography and size band.
inputs.historical_analogs: The analogy is qualitative and does not independently set the selected t50 or role fractions.

inputs.buy_mult: No code-specific, target-band EBITDA transaction source was found in the fresh pass.
inputs.buy_mult: Owner compensation, nonprofit contamination, contract concentration and seasonal field labor require target-level normalization.
inputs.exit_mult: No code-specific exit multiple or observed multiple expansion supports this selection.
inputs.exit_mult: The assumed expansion may disappear if AI pressure or federal demand shocks reduce durable earnings.
inputs.pricing_structure: FAR is not evidence that a majority of target-firm revenue uses this contract type.
source_review.summary: All deterministic mechanics pass and all five registered URLs were freshly accessible. Four score-driving sources support the stated mechanisms or directional claims but remain disclosed proxies rather than direct measurements of NAICS 541720 task shares, adoption, pricing mix, moat, or consolidator activity. The cautious low-confidence kill conclusion remains useful, with all 20 authored caveats carried forward verbatim and no validator-authored material finding.

The corrected 541219 scope and the direction of the pressured, low-capture thesis are supported, with no reversed factor semantics or material capture/terminal double count. All eleven registered sources resolve; the ten score-driving sources support the cited facts or the disclosed direction of estimate inputs. The thesis remains useful, and no material defect is likely to change the factor direction or kill conclusion, but the authored scope, proxy, adoption, pass-through, succession, and multiple-basis caveats must remain visible.

sources.what_it_supports: More than 35% of fashion and luxury executives report using generative AI in selected functions including image creation and product discovery; fashion companies are applying automation to routine work. sources.title: Upwork, Best Freelance Fashion Designers for Hire

sources.title: U.S. Bank, 2025 Small Business Perspective Survey
sources.what_it_supports: National survey of 1,000 U.S. businesses with two to 99 employees and up to $25M revenue: more than half of owners are over 55 and only 54% report a succession plan. Used only as a general size-band owner-age proxy.

sources.title: Vogue, The State of Fashion M&A in 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Fashion deal coverage centers on brand, retail, licensing and portfolio transactions, with conservative valuations amid macro pressure. Used to explain why fashion-brand M&A cannot be treated as evidence of a 541490 design-services roll-up market.

inputs.active_consolidators: Ranked firms are not all active acquirers in every local market.

inputs.exit_mult: Exit depends on recurring maintenance mix, management depth and buyer-market conditions; no disclosed band-size transaction series supports a precise spread.
inputs.pricing_structure: Public and LM150 sources overrepresent larger commercial operators.
source_review.summary: Mechanics and exact-artifact binding pass. Reopened sources support the modest-AI, physical-work and fragmented-market thesis, and no factor semantics or capture/terminal double counting is reversed. The record remains useful as a kill conclusion, but its adoption, consolidator, capture and multiple inputs rely on explicitly disclosed estimates and broad comparators, so the authored caveats must remain visible.

The sources support the pressed-but-surviving demand thesis, current AI-use direction, succession gap, one strategic acquisition, and the stated valuation anchors. publication_caveats: inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The supplied 4-digit 5614 occupations are a poor proxy for 561492 and omit court reporters/stenographers.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Freelance and 1099 reporter payouts may be a larger production cost than the W-2 wage-based labor-share input.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The role arithmetic is required against the supplied proxy and is not observed 561492 occupational data.
inputs.pi_dist: Federal court rates are a pricing-mechanism reference, not a complete representation of private deposition pricing.
inputs.pi_moat: No target-specific reporter agreements, rule coverage or client retention data was reviewed.
inputs.t50_years: AAERT documents a digital path, not a measured 561492 AI-adoption rate.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: This is effective-work adoption, not the percentage of firms using any AI tool.
inputs.historical_analogs: Digital reporting is not equivalent to fully autonomous certification.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: Reporter age is not the same as business-owner age.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct.succession_shortage_documented: This is evidence of a reporter pipeline shortage, not a census of agency ownership succession plans.
inputs.active_consolidators: This is not a paid transaction-database count and active status varies by geography and target quality.
inputs.buy_mult: S4 is a commercial valuation guide rather than a disclosed-comps database; the dataset's $1-10M EBITDA band spans regional and platform categories.
inputs.exit_mult: Exit depends on client/roster transferability and is not a guaranteed multiple expansion; source ranges are commercial estimates.
inputs.pricing_structure: Federal court schedules do not establish a universal private deposition price or agency margin.

Validator caveat: reporter retirement and production-talent shortage are proxies for agency-owner age and ownership succession; this modestly raises B but does not change the kill verdict or conditional thesis.
source_review.summary: Mechanics, exact-artifact identity, source coverage and arithmetic pass. The sources support the industry boundary, digital-reporting pathway, pricing mechanism, commercial multiple ranges and consolidation direction. The owner/succession use of reporter-workforce evidence is a non-fatal disclosed proxy that modestly affects B but cannot change the kill verdict or useful conditional thesis; no material numeric-support, factor-direction or capture/terminal double-counting defect was found.

source_review.summary: The exact attempt-2 packet, finalized run and memo pass schema, identity, routing, frozen-digest, dataset, deterministic-rerender and arithmetic checks. Both registered NAR sources are accessible and support the material reported adoption and consumer-agent-use facts. The 42% adoption input is a valid calculation from mutually exclusive reported frequency tiers, while its interpretation as effective firm adoption is candidly disclosed as a proxy. Several buyability, capture and multiple inputs remain low-confidence estimates rather than source-observed values, but their missing code-specific census, owner, transaction-comparable and pricing-series evidence is expressly caveated, ranges are provided, factor semantics remain correct, capture is separated from terminal demand, and the weak-acquisition conclusion remains useful.

inputs.active_consolidators: No authoritative registry in the consulted source set counts active dental consolidators; the selected count is a broad estimate and the formula caps its effect.

inputs.buy_mult: S8 is an advisory-market compilation, not a transaction database; actual price varies sharply by specialty, owner-production dependence, geography and deal structure. The deterministic $1–10M target definition spans multiple S8 bands, while the sole class counted in n_band averages about $3.196M EBITDA.

inputs.exit_mult: This is a size-matched directional estimate, not evidence of an obtainable future exit multiple. A larger post-aggregation business could occupy a different size band, but no deterministic exit size is supplied, so the packet does not claim that uplift.
inputs.pricing_structure: No national target-band payer-mix split is used; the statement deliberately does not assign a single observed mix across this heterogeneous code.

dataset_inputs.n_band: The 369-firm estimate uses a 15.8% hospitals/healthcare-facilities margin proxy and employment-class averages; it is a floor rather than a census because some 20-99-employee dental groups may exceed $1 million of normalized EBITDA.

inputs.buy_mult / inputs.exit_mult: S8's 9x-11x band is for platform-grade regional groups; the deterministic size proxy establishes average EBITDA but not the platform attributes required for that band, so 10x remains directional.

source_review.summary: Publishable with caveats. Exact identity, schema, model route, frozen digests, deterministic dataset injection, fresh finalization, memo rendering and arithmetic all pass. Reopened sources support the durable-demand and bounded administrative-automation thesis, and the corrected equal 10x entry and exit multiples remove any unsupported expansion from M. The remaining owner-age, succession, consolidator, payer-mix, adoption, target-count and valuation inputs are disclosed estimates or proxies; none changes the record's M=0, S=0 kill conclusion.

sources.title: What are the standards for AI use in dentistry?
sources.what_it_supports: ADA describes dental AI applications including image analysis and nonclinical claims, payment-integrity and quality-assurance areas, and the need for validation and responsible integration.

sources.title: FDA 510(k) summary K242600: Pearl Second Opinion PC
sources.what_it_supports: A current FDA-cleared dental image-analysis product aids radiograph review and detection and is explicitly not a replacement for a dentist's complete review or clinical judgment.

sources.title: AI in Dentistry: 2024 Heartland-ADHA survey
sources.what_it_supports: Survey respondents reported 8.2% of their practices already used dental AI and 50.2% were not at all prepared for adoption; it is directional evidence on current adoption and readiness.

sources.title: ADA responds to HHS request for information on AI adoption in dentistry
sources.what_it_supports: ADA says adoption remains uneven, particularly in small and mid-sized practices, and identifies data, interoperability, privacy and regulatory barriers.

sources.title: ADA HPI dental practice research
sources.what_it_supports: ADA HPI reports 72.5% private-practice ownership as of 2023, 16% dentist DSO affiliation as of 2024, and a continuing shift toward larger practices and DSOs.

sources.title: Dental Industry Predictions for 2025
sources.what_it_supports: ADA HPI's chief economist describes a generational transition and about one percentage point per year moving from solo toward group practice, while discussing private-equity affiliated practices.

sources.title: Dear ADA: Reimbursement rates
sources.what_it_supports: ADA reports that reimbursement rates are not keeping pace with inflation and practice expenses and discusses payer contracts, fee schedules, write-offs and negotiation.

sources.title: Dental Practice Valuation & EBITDA Multiples
sources.what_it_supports: A current advisory-market compilation describes size-stratified adjusted-EBITDA ranges: 5–7x for $500K–$1M, 7–9x for $1M–$3M, 9–11x for $3M–$5M+ and higher platform bands; it is directional rather than a transaction database.

sources.title: Medicaid Fee Schedules
sources.what_it_supports: ADA compiles state-specific dental Medicaid fee-for-service schedules and warns that schedules are state-specific, regularly updated and may omit payment streams.

sources.what_it_supports: The FY2025 President’s Budget requested $2.2 billion for the National Nanotechnology Initiative across 12 agencies, with more than $45 billion cumulative NNI funding since 2001.

sources.title: SBIR.gov Awards

sources.title: How to Value a Federal Government Contracting Business in 2026 — ExitValue.ai
sources.what_it_supports: Public government-contracting valuation commentary identifies cleared workforce, prime contracts, contract vehicles, DCAA compliance and customer diversification as multiple drivers; used only as a lower-middle-market GovCon valuation proxy.

source_review.summary: Publishable with caveats. Exact mechanics, identity, model routing, manifest digests, deterministic dataset injection, fresh finalization, memo rendering and arithmetic all pass. Every registered source is accessible: Adobe supports the 40.9% daily-use proxy but not representative effective adoption or t50; BLS and Upwork support the core task, pricing and demand-risk directions; the M&A and succession sources are broad proxies whose limits and estimate status are disclosed. The packet avoids the prior adoption-segment aggregation error, keeps t50 positive below the chosen proxy boundary, preserves the contaminated 4-digit role mix rather than mutating it, and presents a useful low-confidence kill screen. The capture/exit/terminal mechanisms overlap enough to warrant an additional visible double-counting caveat, but the reopened evidence does not show that this overlap alone is likely to reverse the thesis without also changing multiple independent low-retention and exit judgments.

sources.title: Adobe, How Creatives are Thinking About AI
sources.what_it_supports: June 2026 survey of nearly 2,000 creatives: 38% daily AI use overall; graphic designers lead the listed roles with 40.9% daily use; the largest segment is mostly weekly users. Used for adoption timing and workflow exposure, not NAICS revenue share.

sources.title: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Graphic Designers Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.what_it_supports: Defines graphic-design tasks such as layouts, logos, concepts, client revisions and final production; reports 2% projected employment growth for 2024–34 and notes AI may reduce companies' need to contract with freelance graphic designers.

sources.title: Upwork, Graphic Designer Hourly Rates
sources.what_it_supports: Marketplace indication of $15–$35 median hourly rates, a wider $15–$150 range, and common fixed-price engagements. Used only to characterize transparent, project-based pricing and global freelance alternatives.

sources.title: J&A Sell-Side M&A Report, Independent Creative Agencies M&A Transactions and Valuations
sources.what_it_supports: Broad independent-creative-agency M&A context through Q4 2024, including the distinction between technology-driven agencies and traditional service models and examples of strategic acquisitions. It is not a pure-play 541430 small-firm valuation comp.

sources.title: Gallup, Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.what_it_supports: Fall-2024 survey of U.S. business owners showing nonemployer businesses are less likely to expect a sale or transfer and more likely to expect closure or uncertainty. Used only as generic small-owner succession context.

source_review.summary: All deterministic mechanics and frozen digests pass, and all three registered sources are live. IRS Free File and H&R Block's filed channel, AI-assistance, franchise, and refund-product evidence support the useful conclusion that simple consumer preparation is highly automatable but offers weak capture and consolidation economics; GAO confirms Direct File is suspended. Publication requires the authored caveats because adoption above 50%, role weights, independent-firm economics, owner demographics, consolidator count, and multiples are disclosed estimates or broad proxies rather than code-level observations. No inaccessible, contradicted, or materially misrepresented score-driving evidence warrants rejection.

source_review.summary: Mechanics and artifact binding pass, all seven registered sources are accessible, and the cited observations support the thesis direction. The score-driving estimates rely on broad, old, vendor, or single-provider proxies, but those limitations are explicit, the affected inputs are classified as low-confidence estimates with ranges, and no undisclosed contradiction, reversed semantics, or capture/terminal double count was found. The conclusion remains useful only with all authored caveats preserved.

s5.rationale: Fixed transform of the disclosed v3 owner-age range into five-year qualifying control-transfer propensity.

a.rationale: Assistive AI materially automates editing, culling, file preparation and administration, but physical capture, creative direction and client-facing production remain partly human. Fractions measure technically automatable task share, not layoffs or demand substitution.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541922 ($721,209,516) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541922 ($2,481,739,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

rho.rationale: Fixed outcome-blind transform of the v3 t50 adoption estimate, widened by the declared bridge error.

publication_caveats: inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Supplied role mix is 5-digit 54192 and is shared with portrait studios; retail and process shares need not represent commercial studios.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Fractions separate task automation from terminal demand; generative substitution is assessed in terminal value rather than used to inflate V.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The residual wage bucket likely contains production assistants and digital technicians whose mix differs by commercial subsegment.
inputs.pi_dist: No reopened source measures the mix of day rate, production fee and usage-license revenue for NAICS 541922.
inputs.pi_dist: Agency compensation evidence is a buyer-side proxy, not a commercial-studio contract census.
inputs.pi_moat: Copyright and consent constraints are not a general pricing moat and are not counted as terminal protection twice.
inputs.pi_moat: Client relationship ownership is target-specific and may sit with the individual photographer rather than the studio.
inputs.t50_years: Adobe is a global creator survey, Aftershoot samples photographers who already integrated AI, and Census is economy-wide; none is a NAICS 541922 effective-adoption census.
inputs.t50_years: The range can move A materially and crosses the Pass/Conditional neighborhood when combined with other uncertainty.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: No direct U.S. commercial-photography firm survey was reopened.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: Tool use, post-processing automation and firm-wide effective adoption are different constructs.
inputs.historical_analogs: No code-specific historical technology-adoption series was reopened.
inputs.historical_analogs: The analogue is directional and is not an independent forecast of revenue substitution.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: Not an observed commercial-photography owner-age statistic; the input should not be read as demographic evidence.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct.succession_shortage_documented: Absence of reopened documentation is not evidence that no succession issue exists.
inputs.active_consolidators: Not a census: undiscovered local buyers or adjacent media consolidators could exist.
inputs.active_consolidators: This input is used only in B and is not evidence for V or A.
inputs.buy_mult: BizBuySell's SDE basis includes owner economics and is not interchangeable with normalized EBITDA.
inputs.buy_mult: Communication and Media is broader than commercial photography and the reported transactions are generally smaller than the requested EBITDA band.
inputs.exit_mult: No direct commercial-photography EBITDA exit series was reopened.
inputs.exit_mult: A target with recurring contracts and management depth could differ materially, but that cannot be inferred from this industry record.
inputs.pricing_structure: No reopened NAICS 541922 pricing survey quantified the revenue mix.
inputs.pricing_structure: Usage licensing can preserve value for authentic capture while being especially exposed where a client accepts synthetic imagery.

cross_checks.terminal_value: Registered sources establish generative-image capability and authenticity constraints, but not the share of NAICS 541922 revenue exposed to synthetic substitution; MELTING remains a low-confidence directional judgment rather than measured demand evidence.
source_review.summary: All deterministic mechanics, identity, route, dataset, fresh-finalization, memo, digest, and arithmetic checks pass. All seven registered score-driving sources were reopened and provide useful directional support, but the evidence is mostly broader than NAICS 541922 and does not directly quantify several selected estimates. No material defect was found; the kill thesis remains publishable with the authored caveats and the added non-fatal terminal-demand evidence caveat.

sources.title: AI & Photography: A Field Guide for ASMP Members (2025)
sources.what_it_supports: ASMP describes generative image creation, assistive editing uses, client disclosure and licensing practices, and the continuing importance of substantial human authorship.

sources.title: Adobe Creators' Toolkit Report announcement
sources.what_it_supports: Adobe reports a global survey of more than 16,000 creators in which 86% actively used creative generative AI; editing, enhancement and new asset generation were leading uses.

sources.title: 4As State of GenAI Adoption 2025
sources.what_it_supports: The advertising-agency survey reports 75% of respondents using GenAI, production efficiency expectations, and compensation-model pressure from time-saving AI.

sources.title: The Microstructure of AI Diffusion: Evidence from Firms, Business Functions, and Worker Tasks
sources.what_it_supports: Census BTOS research reports 18% firm AI use in a business function in late 2025/early 2026, higher use in professional services, and limited breadth of use among adopters.

sources.title: The Great Photography Workflow Shift Revolution
sources.what_it_supports: Aftershoot's 2025 survey of photographers already using AI reports heavy post-processing automation and expectations of further post-production automation.

sources.title: Copyright Office Releases Part 2 of Artificial Intelligence Report
sources.what_it_supports: The U.S. Copyright Office says AI outputs can be protected only where a human author determines sufficient expressive elements, while AI assistance does not itself bar copyrightability.

sources.title: Business Valuation Multiples by Industry
sources.what_it_supports: BizBuySell reports trailing-five-year Main Street transaction benchmarks, explains that reported earnings multiples are seller's discretionary earnings, and reports a 2.47x Communication and Media sector earnings multiple.

cross_checks.terminal_value: S2 and S3 establish authorized acts and qualifications in two states but do not establish national exclusivity or durable paid demand for 541120; the DURABLE terminal class remains a low-confidence inference.
source_review.summary: Publishable with caveats. Exact identity, model routing, schema, dataset injection, byte-identical finalization and memo rendering, and arithmetic all pass. Census supports the corrected narrow 541120 boundary, and the registered sources support their expressly limited legal-authority, adoption-proxy and price-anchor facts. The zero-target conclusion remains useful as a low-confidence feasibility screen, not a measured market statistic: its office-count, revenue, margin, ownership and multiple inputs are openly judgmental, while the labor and adoption evidence are broad disclosed proxies. The state statutes also do not independently prove durable national paid demand, so the terminal label requires the additional validator caveat above. None of these limitations can reverse the kill thesis driven by zero B and M under the selected estimates.
